findings.summary: The Titanic dataset contains 891 passengers with 12 features. The target is binary (Survived: 38.4% positive rate). Sex is the strongest predictor (r=+0.543 for female), followed by Pclass (r=-0.338) and Fare (r=+0.257). Critical data quality issues: Cabin is 77.1% missing and Age is 19.9% missing. Fare is extremely right-skewed (skew=4.79) and requires log transformation. Key pattern: females survived at 74.2% vs males at 18.9%; 1st class at 63% vs 3rd class at 24.2%.

key_patterns: Sex is the dominant predictor: females 74.2% vs males 18.9% survival rate
Sex x Pclass interaction is highly informative
HasCabin is a strong proxy feature (66.7% vs 30.0% survival)
Cherbourg embarkation associated with higher survival (55.4%)
Moderate class imbalance: 61.6% non-survivors vs 38.4% survivors

recommendations.action: Create Title feature from Name column (Mr., Mrs., Miss., Master., etc.)
Create HasCabin binary flag and Deck extraction from Cabin column
Create FamilySize = SibSp + Parch + 1 and IsAlone binary flag
Apply log1p transform to Fare to correct extreme skew (skew=4.79)
Impute Age using median stratified by Pclass and Sex (train-only)
Impute Embarked with mode (Southampton) — only 2 missing values
Use stratified train/test split and class_weight='balanced' for models
Drop PassengerId and raw Name/Ticket/Cabin columns before modeling

next_steps: Run ML theory review for model selection
Implement ensemble models (Random Forest, XGBoost; expected AUC: 0.88-0.92)
